Article.ArticleTitle: Mechanical properties and heavy metal environmental safety of incineration fly ash mortar under sulfate erosion.

Abstract.AbstractText: Incineration fly ash has the feasibility of being used as the cement admixture, and heavy metals can be solidified by hydration products. However, there is little research on the solidification ability of heavy metals in the process of sulfate corrosion of the prepared cement. The solidification ability of mortar to heavy metals in sulfate environment after incineration fly ash as the admixture was evaluated. The results showed that during the process of sulfate erosion, the mass of mortar increased without significant changes in appearance, the compressive strength first increased and then decreased, but all were higher than those of 0 times of sulfate erosion. As the dosage of incineration fly ash increased, the point of decrease in compressive strength appears earlier (from 150 times to 120 times), indicating that incineration fly ash was not conducive to improving the sulfate resistance of cement. Mortar was divided into surface and central parts. The heavy metal properties of the surface part changed significantly, while the heavy metal properties of the central part remain unchanged. The leaching concentration of surface heavy metals increased by 4428.16 %, while the central part only increased by 175.00 %, but both were below the limit. Sulfate erosion changed the types and masses of hydration products, the pore structure of the mortar, and the occurrence state of heavy metals, significantly weakening the solidification ability of hydration products to heavy metals. Therefore, when recycling hazardous waste in cement, its long-term environmental safety needed to be considered.
